text: Recently took a job with lower title, but much better salary and likely better overall career prospects. Does the lower title matter? | Hello all, the title essentially. I recently accepted an offer for a job that is a 31% increase in salary and isa pretty major name in the finance sphere, which is what I want career-wise (whereas right now I am in tech). However, in my current place in tech, I'm a "manager" and at this new job, it's going to be on the "specialist"/"coordinator" level. All things considered, the shift to this job, aside from the title, is a no brainer (bigger company with massive name recognition, higher salary, significantly higher likelihood for advancement, etc). I'm just curious, would the technical downshift in title really pose any issues to me later in the future or look bad on paper, or am I being silly? TIA!

Has anyone had luck in the job market? | Ive been seing a lot of posts recently about layoffs and people sending out 500+ applications with no offers. But has anyone had luck landing a position since being laid off? If so, how long did it take you, whats your YOE, etc.

I am professional, give excellent answers, am eloquent, everything seems great, then I never hear from them again. Not knowing what I should do differently, firmer handshake? $1,000 Armani suit? It feels like every application I complete or interview I have is fake and I'm just there so they say they interviewed someone. 

I've even had am interim CEO who liked me so much that the recommended me for a position (I didn't even ask *him*). He is a hefty donor to the organization and (I think) on the board. I interviewed well for the position then they just told me "[I] don't have enough international experience," whatever the hell that means. Will I ever get a job? I'd get 3 PhDs but I'll just be the most educated underemployed dude in the world!

Any suggestions?

Why did my boss lie on my performance review, and kill my project? | I can understand a boss having a different opinion about how good my performance is, but in this review there was a pretty big bald faced lie - my boss claimed that I had not finished a project. I completed the project based on all the specifications we laid out in the original project planning document (which was signed by my boss!) I gathered evidence the project was working, and had multiple engineers sign off on the testing and validation of the project. 

I am extremely meticulous and document things well. So I showed him the document and asked what specific task I had failed. He said I had not completed a extra task not in the planning document, never communicated to me, and was never brought up for 3 months while I was doing the project. It was only mentioned after I completed the project. 

My coworkers all have similar projects to me, and none of them have done this additional extra task he mentioned.

He didn’t say “the project wasn’t completed fast enough” or “the project wasn’t good enough”, just that it wasn’t complete. 

He also mentioned the project was going to be scrapped because I “couldn’t complete it”, but multiple people told me they still need the module I made and it was important. I also think the task he asked me to do would only take a week at most. I offered to do it, he said no.

I’m get the feeling there’s some nasty office politics stuff involved. Does this scenario seem familiar to any of you? Trying to suss out what could be happening

How do You separate your self-worth from your year-end review? | I’m a really hard worker, but i do struggle with my mental health. I know I’m not perfect and can falter at high-stress times, but I’m really in my head feeling like i do way too much and don’t get the credit i deserve. I was even promoted to a supervisor for 5 months of last year. How do i disconnect from this? Is it fair to hold a handful of bad days in a year against me? (Bad day= break down in tears and need to leave early, sometimes due to work, sometimes due to personal life). I question whether i should even be doing the work I’m doing due to my mental health at times, but it pays well and I’m good at it the majority of the time. I am currently pursuing an HR certificate, because i feel I’d have a better work/life balance and it would help my mental health. Any advice welcomed.

Is "passion" necessary for a successful career? | It seems that when many students are trying to choose a career path and what they want to do in the future, the question of passion often rises. Nowadays, it is even condemned sometimes if you don't have this "bright of passion" and only choose a career based on earning potential(which is surprising in our current economic situation).

There'd be the occasional good role in there, but either I'd decide not to pursue after an interview or two bc things just weren't as good as they were on paper, or I wouldn't get a response after initial call (I usually got the impression they were looking for something different than what the job description was saying).

But lately, she's sending me a lot of requests for updates to my resume. Things like going into detail on my Agile experience, or asking me to add things that are much more devops related or, related to some other aspect of tech that I really don't touch. I understand keywords are a thing, but apparently just listing the skills isn't enough.

I didn't yell at her or chew her out or anything. But I did lay out clearly that there are just some things that I feel waste space on a resume, and I don't want to update my resume every other day just to get new buzzwords on there.

UPDATE: After hundreds of applications I was finally offered a position. Are my salary expectations too high? | A little over 2 months ago I posted that I received my first offer. 20 per hour for 2 months, than salary at 50k. I took what seemed to be the advice of the majority, accepting the position, but continued to apply for other positions.

Most recommended to not add the new job to my resume, but I did anyway. And personally, I think the added real work experience helped my resume IMMENSELY. Before that I would get around 1 response for an interview per 100 applications. After adding the work experience, I applied to ~50 applications, and had 11 responses for interviews. I was genuinely shocked how frequently I was being called to schedule an interview.

Lots of people mentioned that it was likely to be a bait n switch starting at 20 per hour then after two months they wouldn't move me to salary at 50k. For me, this was not the case. They did sent me a promotion contract agreeing to the new salary change. The salaried devs on the team do not clock in, and are able to work from home when they want to, and I assumed that would be the case for me and the other junior devs they hired. When I accepted the raise and asked about it, I was told that not only do I have to still clock in (for keeping track of metrics for their clients) but that I was not allowed to work from home at all.

Seeing how many more interviews I was receiving, I was able to realize that 50k with the restrictions is definitely a low ball for the Austin TX area. I began to take applying to other positions more seriously. I am incredibly glad that I decided to take this position though, because the experience I learned in just 2 months really elevated my knowledge of the field, and I was able to secure a remote position at 70k. With my 2 weeks put in, I'm super happy with the 40% raise I acquired by job hopping. 75% raise if you consider the position I accepted originally was 20 an hour. 

I'd like to thank you guys for the original advice, and if you have any questions for me feel free to ask!

UPDATE to the update:

My employer I gave the 2 weeks notice to sent me home today. Guess I have an unpaid 2 week vacation :\^)

Passed over for a promotion and I’m gutted. What now? | Hi All, 

Looking for some advice on processing being passed over for a promotion. 

I have been in this role for two years, in the industry for seven and today I was passed over for a promotion I already see myself as over-qualified for by a recent grad in his first job. I say that not in an entitled sense, but because I took the role I’m currently in because the last role I had was technically higher and at a more prestigious firm, but the politics there burned me out, so I took this one as an escape route.

We have both been in this role for about two years. He started two months before me, but at a lower rank and was promoted to my level about two months after I started. Out of my team of 8, we both have stand-out work ethics in comparison to the rest of the team, but my “desk” is more complex, I log more hours and have taken less vacation, and my metrics show more productivity. I am also more of a point man for the lower levels on the team to come to, run client calls because the VP is always unprepared, and have been the person to run meetings when the VP is out, so I’m completely baffled at this decision. My monthly reviews have been nothing but stellar. I’m often annoyed because the feedback is all positive and gives me nothing constructive to work on.

The VP told me to apply for this position on the internal career portal when it opened up. HR called me the next day and said it was basically down to logistics, and the interview was basically my team lead and I talking about hobbies and nothing technical.

The two of us are very close, and often hang out outside of work, so I’m genuinely happy for him, but it doesn’t take the sting out of being passed over. I go out of my way to build rapport with everyone we work with, volunteer my lunches every Wednesday (sometimes alone) for the food bank we’re partnered with, restarted an internal network for socializing/volunteering that fell apart during covid, and have hosted events featuring partners and C-Suite, while he just coordinates the co-ed softball team. I love my team, the firm, the job and my coworkers, but I’m feeling deflated and under-appreciated and considering taking a job elsewhere. 

I asked directly for feedback, and the department head gave me lip service, saying I’m doing everything right, should continue doing the things I’m doing, and he sees a promotion on the horizon; basically confusing me even more because on paper, I was the shoe-in and don’t have an explanation for being passed over. 

My cousin told me he could get me a job paying about 25k more at our rival, which I’m considering, but I’ve developed relationships here and was enjoying the culture. But it doesn’t spell “future development” to me if I’m being passed over by recent grads in their first role. 

Sorry for the text wall, but I’m shocked and a bit hurt. I’m genuinely happy for my friend, as he is capable, but I don’t feel like working tomorrow now. Do I leave for another opportunity or just stay professional and keep plugging away?

Am I being unprofessional for taking the better offer? | I recently completed my master's degree and have been working at my current job for six years. While wrapping up my studies, I secured an entry-level position within my company, which took a back seat as I fulfilled my duties in my previous role.

On the day of my graduation, I had an interview with a renowned organization in my area, aligning perfectly with my educational background and offering a significantly higher salary. The interview went well, and I followed up with a letter. However, I didn't hear back from them for a few weeks. Meanwhile, I had the chance to attend other interviews, all of which offered higher pay than my potential role with my current employer.

Fast forward to two weeks ago when I received a call from my dream organization, offering me the job. It was an ideal fit for me, both professionally and financially, with a $30k increase compared to what my current employer had offered for the new position. I decided to resign from my current job, informing my manager and the other manager, which would have been my new manager for the entry level position, later in the day.

Unfortunately, the other manager seemed annoyed and reacted unprofessionally, rolling her eyes and criticizing my choices. She called me "unprofessional" for pursuing the opportunity and expressed her satisfaction at my departure. I thanked her for the opportunity, but I explained that the other offer was a better fit for me in terms of proximity to home, personal fulfillment, and financial gain. Despite my explanation, she rudely told me to leave her office. 

Naturally, I feel frustrated by this situation. From an outsider's perspective, I consider myself a professional and respectful individual. It's disheartening to be labeled "unprofessional" for making a move in my life that will greatly benefit me. Furthermore, since I hadn't even started the new role, it wouldn't have had any impact on their operations or required additional training. I would appreciate hearing other perspectives on this matter. Do you think I acted unprofessionally by seizing an opportunity that will greatly benefit me?

How to handle recruiters who say they don’t have a salary range? | It’s clearly a lie. Who doesn’t budget for their hires? But so many companies have done this with me. It’s super annoying. 

I don’t want to answer this question so I usually try to ask them that question in return if they do ask me. But every single time, whether it’s a small company or a large MNC, they lie and say they don’t have a range or budget in mind. If I say it depends on the overall package (leave, benefits, etc), they keep pushing and pushing me to give a number.  Because I really need a job (this is quite a key factor in why I eventually give in), I don’t want to leave a negative impression, and just provide a number, not knowing if I’m over or under quoting. 

Prior to the interview though, I do do due diligence and can sometimes find a similar-ish position listed on job sites by the same company with a listed salary range and quote accordingly - still, it’s not super clear cut since it’s a *similar-ish* position. And sometimes I can’t find anything at all and don’t know what to quote. Salary ranges for the same position can vary wildly (even across similarly-sized companies) so I really hate this question. 

What’s the best way to handle this question?

Need different perspectives, what would you guys do in this situation? | I’m out of college working at rainforest for $180k TC. I’ve been here for ~3 months and honestly have a lot of conflicting opinions on this job.

My team as people are great. Very nice and accepting people. But we are in a very serious environment, we are a VERY core team of AWS, and have many issues with our codebases.

There are constant comments from other teams and ours that this is probably the most complex, large, and fast paced teams in AWS. Multiple comments about how this is not an ideal environment for new engineers to develop. 

I get very, very little help from most people. It has been fine for the most part and I have survived and delivered my tasks. But I constantly feel like I’m behind, I am constantly overwhelmed, and every morning for the first 2 hours I honestly just want to die and never come back to the job again.

I feel like my team and manager has given me tasks that were too important, with 0 help or guidance. Then I get pressured and constantly asked when it will be done when im taking too long on it. I work 9am-10pm maybe once a week to get things delivered. We have a team of 30+ so it seems really easy for people to think “eh whatever someone else will help him”

I get paid a lot, but at this point working at a fast food chain and being broke sounds better. Another thing that bothers me is I feel so embarrassed of my performance. Every day in standup and everytime we go to office. My team has 30+ people, all more experienced than me, and most people (25 probably) came from overseas. My manager, other manager, and my skip did as well. Because of this I feel the cultures collide and people look at me in a different light. “Spoiled white kid who grew up in America” and doesn’t work as hard as hard. I think people think I’m slacking when I’m not. I feel very out of place and very overwhelmed every morning and night.

Thoughts?

I had a great interview yesterday, they called me back two hours later asking to meet for 30 minutes to go over salary and benefits, good sign that I got the job? | For context, this is an entry-level environmental scientist position with a consulting firm that I'm applying for. I have a B.S. in Geoscience, a minor in OSHA, I'm a member of the Women in Geosciences organization, and I have about 3 combined years of experience in wastewater and lab analysis work and undergraduate hydrology research. 

So this is the fastest I've ever seen a hiring process go. I applied for a position last week through LinkedIn, they viewed my resume two days later, and emailed me the same day asking to set up a pre-screening phone call. I took the call on Thursday and a lady from HR ran through my resume, seemed like a basic check just to make sure I wasn't lying about my qualifications. A few hours later, she calls me back and says she's passed my resume onto the hiring committee and they want me for a Teams interview on Tuesday (yesterday). I think I killed the interview tbh; It was a 45-minutes and I had them laughing with jokes, they seemed very fascinated with my lab experience, impressed with the background research I did on the company and position, and it turns out that they actually have a whole work group dedicated to one of my passion projects in undergrad, microplastics. Plus, I really clicked with the guy who would I would be working with if I did get the position! At the very end, they asked me what I would expect from an employer, and I kind of ran down the work hierarchy, working conditions, and safety in field, but just blanked on talking about salary and benefits since I didn't want to ruin the mood (it sounds silly I know, but I didn't want to interrupt all this science with money 😅). 

But I figured that if the interview didn't go over as well as I thought, I'd probably just see a nice rejection email a few weeks later and move on. Yet lo and behold, two hours later the same HR lady calls me to set up a 30 minutes Teams on Wednesday (today) with the hiring manager I spoke with to discuss salary and benefits! Now usually I wouldn't get my hopes up, but I told my mom about this and she said they wouldn't have bothered calling to talk about salary if they didn't want me. And if they're moving this quick, it means I must be the best candidate in the pool (The position has been posted for a month I believe, 32 applicants, at least on LinkedIn, but I'm sure some of them got turned away during the pre-screen). She's already convinced I have the job. And considering that it looks like this is a new branch of a 70-year-old consulting firm, it'd make sense that they're trying to get a fresh, qualified face in the door as quick as possible. And she makes good points and is usually never wrong about things like this, but the skeptic in me is reeling. I have the 30 minute chat today at 12:30, so I'll probably have a clearer answer by the end of that, but some reassurances or reality checks would be appreciated.
